description: Exposure can hurt even when no one else speaks. 'nupira' is the self-conscious pain of being rejected or diminished in one's own eyes or in the eyes of others. It may accompany responsibility for an act, but it can also grow from mistaken or imposed condemnation. 'thiku nila' (seeing as small) is contempt directed at someone; 'nupira' is the feeling carried by the person who experiences shame.
articulatory_notes: Place the tongue tip against the backs of the upper teeth for voiced dental 'n', carry the voice through the nose, and round the lips around high-back 'u'. Close the lips for unvoiced 'p' and release into stressed high-front unrounded 'i'. Make a tap or short trill at the alveolar ridge for 'r', then finish with open central 'a'.
sound_symbolism: The rounded nasal opening draws close, the lips close completely at the stressed centre, and the final tap releases into open 'a'. In Phi, that movement may echo the pull between wanting to withdraw and feeling exposed.
usage_notes: 'nupira' takes 'phaelo' (feel) as other feeling nouns do. A person may also release it with 'leiro' (release); neither construction decides whether the judgement that prompted the shame was fair.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Shame can be observed with compassion as a changing experience rather than accepted as a fixed identity.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Repair can pass through shame without using humiliation as its method. Naming the feeling leaves conduct, effect, and amends available for separate speech.

semantic_domains.community: self-conscious pain shaped by exposure or social judgement
semantic_domains.emotion: the painful feeling of being diminished or exposed
